text: Hi, welcome to This is My Architecture. I'm Sunay from AWS. And with me today, I have Deepak
from Accenture. Deepak, welcome on the show. Thanks. So Deepak, we are here today to talk
about your Circulus Apply Chain Solution. Why don't you take us through what customer problem
does the solution solve? Yep. So you love your coffee, right? So having a coffee is a ritual.
The smell, the rose, the brew, and it's priceless. But is it? Because the price you pay for it,
only 5% of it goes to the farmer. There are like 500 million that small scale farmers who
don't, who live on $2 or less of their income, and they don't get the due credit. So there
is a misalignment of incentives to them. And our circular supply chain helps us solve that
using three technologies, blockchain, digital identity, and payments to ensure that the right
people in the supply chain get the right incentives to do what they want to do. And the big thing
is the sustainability here. Brilliant. I mean, it's an important problem to solve. The farmer
gets incentivized for his work, right? So could you also walk us through how you are solving
that problem in the diagram here? So we talked about sustainability, and we want to monitor
how that's done. And the big part is IoTs. So there is a lot of IoTs being put in to monitor
those at different farm lines and stuff like that. So the data comes in, we have put a threshold.
So we need to ensure that there is a threshold taken. And the thresholds are then written into
the lambda function, then the function is called, it goes into the application load balancer, and then
writes it into my blockchain running on my EKS service. And here we are using hyperledger fabric
with smart contracts, and stuff like that. And at the parallel, in parallel, what happens is as soon as
it's written on to blockchain, we write it on off chain on DynamoDB. And there are a few cases where,
and I want to take an example here is, there is a smart contract, which gets invoked for a few of the
events where we would like to use our SNS service. Take an example, right? So there is this truck taking a
produce, and you have an IoT running that it needs to be refrigerated, or in the temperature needs to be
below 25 degrees. So if there is a threshold which reaches above it, the produce becomes
not valuable, and you need to throw that. So why do you want to go through the entire chain, take it to the
manufacturing unit? So you could then use this invoked smart contract, send a notification to the
driver and its users to ensure that you don't have to do the last mile, just take a U-turn and go to the
closest warehouse dump that produce. And then the next set of smart contract kicks in to send a fresh
produce to the manufacturer. So brilliant, you're using IoT to ensure that the environmental conditions
that are required for safe storage of the shipment is taken care of. And you know, the supply chain also
sort of is updated based on those events. Wonderful. So the ingestion point of the data seems to be the
IoT platform. Could you also walk us through how you're using the AWS IoT platform for
ingesting data? Correct. So the two big challenges we have with the IoT is the humongous scale, because
we want to touch the entire world, I can't right size it right now. And the AWS IoT platform helps me
ensure that I scale scale down as and when required. And the second big problem is security. I don't know
if the IoT device is the trusted device is the device which I put in, or someone has hacked into it. And
the platform allows me to identify it and secure all of these. And then of course, take the scale in play
of it. Wonderful. And I see your blockchain being deployed as containers. Could you also take us to
how that was a design decision for you to deploy your blockchain application as containers? Correct. So the
biggest challenge in blockchain is the adoption. And when I take it to our customers, the first question is,
have you right sized it? How do I manage it? Who deploys it for me? Where is the cost coming from? And by
having my hyperledger fabric running on a EQS service, I could then orchestrate it for my customers,
so that I know exactly when to scale it up, scale it down, and ensure the right nodes are available. And then I
monitor it using my cloud watch, right? This helps me ensure that all my customers' nodes are well
monitored. And if there is something going wrong, I can take necessary action. And when there is high
level of transactions coming in from one particular customer, then I could auto scale that particular
node, and then bring it back down when it normalizes. So those are the things which has helped us ensure that
it is a smooth adoption. And price being paper use, so I do not have to ask them to upfront spend a lot of
money. So this is helping us ensure that we can adopt to blockchain very quickly. Wonderful. And
EKS being a managed platform, it takes away the undifferentiated heavy lifting from your side, and
you are focused more on managing your containers, right? Correct. That is brilliant. Also, I see keys here,
and I am assuming security plays an important role in this solution, which has a financial aspect to it
as well. Could you walk us through how security is important? What are you leveraging from AWS?
Yeah, the fundamental technology for blockchain is cryptography. And that works on keys. And hence,
keys become a very important part of the solution. In the recent past, we have heard a lot of hacks
happening. Someone lost about 90 million because he lost his key. Now you can't go back and get that,
right? And that is where we are saying the key management of the entire blockchain solution
is that the key management of the blockchain can be on a cloud HSM, which is a dedicated FIPS compliant
box, which can help you ensure that the keys are fortified in a hardware module, which can't be,
or it's not easy to be misused. And the second part is that because of few compliance and regulatory norms,
they require the keys to be in a certain geography or within their premises. And that is where you could
integrate your cloud HSM keys with their HSM, which is on premise to ensure that there is a smooth
integration between them. Wonderful. And I also see, you know, a DynamoDB. I'm assuming that there is
a lot of transactions which are happening on the block and users are not directly querying the blockchain,
but are essentially routing their queries to DynamoDB. Is that the purpose of DynamoDB here?
Yeah, there are two things, right? We talk about blockchain being transparent and so when we
implement it, we don't want the platform itself being a black box because today the only way I
look at the blockchain transactions or seeing if the block is formed or not is using a explorer or
writing a CLI command to ensure that there is a block, but it is all gibberish because it's all
encrypted. Now I want to make sure that I have a real-time view to it and my customers who are there
want to see what's happening. And that is where we bring in the DynamoDB as an off-chain database,
where on top of it I create my visualizations based on their role. They can see what the
information is and then take necessary action needed. Wonderful. This is great stuff Deepak.
Would you also take us through what are your roadmap items and what are the other AWS services that you're
looking at integrating with? We talked about analytics, the visualization and stuff like that.
We want to take it further. We want to write it on to the S3 bucket and then take it to SageMaker to
create models for our insights, predictive analytics, foresight and stuff like that,
which is our next step, which we are doing so that it becomes a much more predictive
and foresight driven platform. For example, I would want a manufacturer who want to know if my
produce is going to come on time or not, if my produce is going to get spoiled, misrouted. So all
those models can be built in and then necessary actions can be taken. And that is where we are
going to look at SageMaker to create those models. And the second big thing, as I was saying, the
adoption is basically towards managing the hyperledger environment and its nodes. We would like to use
Amazon managed blockchain services to ensure that it becomes a seamless experience for all our customers.
And it's an easy one click kind of a deploy. So that is where our next steps are to ensure that if
SageMaker and managed blockchain comes in play. Wonderful. Thanks a lot Deepak. Thank you for
explaining to us in detail about the circular supply chain solution. And thank you everyone for joining us
today for this session of This is My Architecture.
